Back
March 2026·12 min read·Distributed Systems

On Building Resilient Distributed Systems

Distributed systems fail. That's not a flaw — it's a fundamental property of any system composed of independent components communicating over a network. The question isn't whether your system will experience partial failure, but whether it degrades gracefully when it does.

"The network is reliable" is the first of the eight fallacies of distributed computing. Every architecture decision you make should be stress-tested against its negation.

Designing for Failure Modes

Resilience starts before a single line of code is written. The most important exercise in distributed system design is enumerating failure modes — not to prevent them, but to understand how your system behaves when they occur.

For every component in your architecture, ask: what happens when this is unavailable? What happens when it's slow? What happens when it returns incorrect results? The answers shape your circuit breakers, your retry logic, your fallback paths.

The Role of Idempotency

Retries are essential in distributed systems. Networks fail, services restart, timeouts happen. But retrying non-idempotent operations creates data consistency problems that are significantly harder to fix than the original failure.

Design operations to be safe to repeat. Use idempotency keys for mutations. Build consumers that handle duplicate messages gracefully. This isn't defensive programming — it's foundational to building systems that can self-heal.

Observability as Infrastructure

You cannot reason about system behavior you cannot observe. Distributed tracing, structured logging, and fine-grained metrics aren't optional in complex systems — they're as important as the system itself.

At Nest, we treat observability as a first-class concern from the first deployment. It's significantly harder to retrofit than to build in from the start.
